items.title: Signed malware impersonating workplace apps deploys RMM backdoors
items.description: Multiple phishing campaigns were identified using workplace meeting lures, PDF attachments, and abuse of legitimate binaries to deliver signed malware. The attacks used digitally signed executables masquerading as legitimate software to install remote monitoring and management (RMM) tools like ScreenConnect, Tactical RMM, and Mesh Agent. These tools enabled attackers to establish persistence and m
items.ai_summary: The threat involves signed malware impersonating workplace apps to deploy RMM backdoors, allowing attackers to establish persistence and remote access. This is achieved through phishing campaigns using meeting lures and PDF attachments. The most important defender action is to implement robust email filtering and user education to prevent initial phishing attempts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1036| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Securing the Supply Chain: How SentinelOne's AI EDR Stops the ...
items.description: On March 31, 2026, a North Korean state actor hijacked the npm credentials of the primary Axios maintainer and published two backdoored releases that deployed a cross-platform remote access trojan (RAT) to Windows, macOS, and Linux systems. Axios is the most widely used HTTP client in the JavaScript ecosystem, with approximately 100 million weekly downloads and a presence in roughly 80% of cloud a
items.ai_summary: The threat is a supply chain compromise where a North Korean state actor hijacked the npm credentials of the primary Axios maintainer, deploying a cross-platform remote access trojan to Windows, macOS, and Linux systems. The real-world impact is significant due to Axios' widespread use in the JavaScript ecosystem. The most important defender action is to immediately update Axios to a version not affected by the backdoor.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A Technique-Based Approach to Hunting Web-Delivered Malware
items.description: This report presents a technique-based approach to HTTP body hunting using Censys that addresses this tension directly, and demonstrates its effectiveness by walking through a live discovery: a ClickFix campaign delivering XWorm V5.6 through a 5-stage attack chain.
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a 5-stage attack chain delivering XWorm V5.6 malware through a ClickFix campaign, utilizing web-delivered malware tactics. The real-world impact includes potential data breaches and system compromises. The most important defender action is to implement robust HTTP body inspection and vulnerability scanning to detect similar threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1595.002| B[Discovery]:::tactic
    B -->|T1566| C[Initial Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: DPRK-Related Campaigns with LNK and GitHub C2
items.description: FortiGuard Labs recently detected a series of LNK files targeting users in South Korea. These attacks use a multi-stage scripting process and leverage GitHub as Command and Control (C2) infrastructure to evade detection. Although these LNK files can be traced back to 2024, earlier versions had less obfuscation and contained significant metadata, allowing us to track similar attacks spreading the X
items.ai_summary: The DPRK-related campaign uses LNK files with a multi-stage scripting process, leveraging GitHub as C2 infrastructure to evade detection. This campaign has been active since 2024, with earlier versions containing less obfuscation and metadata. Defenders should monitor GitHub for suspicious activity and implement C2 infrastructure detection.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1562| B[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    D -->|T1104| E[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Iranian APT Infrastructure in Focus: Mapping State-Aligned Clusters During Geopolitical Escalation
items.description: The analysis examines Iranian state-aligned threat actors and their infrastructure patterns during heightened geopolitical tensions. It focuses on mapping network infrastructure, ASN patterns, TLS fingerprints, and hosting clusters associated with various Iranian APT groups. The report highlights the importance of proactive infrastructure monitoring to detect and disrupt potential cyber operations
items.ai_summary: Iranian state-aligned threat actors exploit geopolitical tensions to map and disrupt potential cyber operations by gathering victim host info and stealing or forging authentication certificates.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1592| B[Gather Victim Host Info]:::tactic
    B -->|T1649| C[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: MuddyWater Exposed: Inside an Iranian APT operation
items.description: Researchers identified and analyzed exposed infrastructure of MuddyWater, an Iranian cyber espionage group linked to the Ministry of Intelligence and Security. The investigation revealed their reconnaissance methods, exploitation of vulnerabilities, custom command and control frameworks, and exfiltration techniques. Targets included organizations in Israel, Jordan, Egypt, UAE, Portugal, and the US
items.ai_summary: MuddyWater, an Iranian APT, uses reconnaissance, exploits vulnerabilities, and custom command and control frameworks to exfiltrate data from targets in multiple countries. Their tactics include Application Layer Protocol and Exfiltration Over C2 Channel. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for these TTPs to prevent data breaches.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Reconnaissance]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: South American telecommunication providers targeted with three new malware implants
items.description: UAT-9244, a China-nexus advanced persistent threat actor, has been targeting critical telecommunications infrastructure in South America since 2024. The group employs three new malware implants: TernDoor, a Windows-based backdoor variant of CrowDoor; PeerTime, an ELF-based backdoor using BitTorrent protocol; and BruteEntry, a brute force scanner for SSH, Postgres, and Tomcat servers. UAT-9244 uses
items.ai_summary: UAT-9244, a China-nexus APT, targets South American telecoms with three new malware implants: TernDoor, PeerTime, and BruteEntry. These implants enable backdoor access, brute force attacks, and remote access. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for T1190, T1110, and T1219 TTPs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1110| C[Brute Force]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: An Investigation Into Years of Undetected Operations Targeting High-Value Sectors
items.description: Since 2020, a Chinese threat actor dubbed CL-UNK-1068 has been targeting high-value organizations across South, Southeast and East Asia, focusing on critical sectors like aviation, energy, government, and telecommunications. The group employs a diverse toolkit including custom malware, modified open-source utilities, and living-off-the-land binaries to maintain stealthy persistence. Their techniqu
items.ai_summary: CL-UNK-1068, a Chinese threat actor, has been undetected since 2020, targeting high-value sectors in Asia with custom malware, modified open-source utilities, and living-off-the-land binaries. Their techniques include exploiting public-facing applications and developing capabilities. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for T1587 and T1190 to detect potential CL-UNK-1068 activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1587| B[Develop Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic

items.title: Middle East Conflict Fuels Opportunistic Cyber Attacks
items.description: The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has triggered a surge in cybercriminal activity. Over 8,000 newly registered domains with conflict-related keywords have been identified, many of which may be weaponized in future campaigns. Multiple cases of malicious activity have been observed, including targeted attacks using conflict-themed lures, deployment of the LOTUSLITE backdoor, fake news blogs le
items.ai_summary: Cybercriminals are exploiting the Middle East conflict to launch opportunistic attacks, using conflict-themed lures and newly registered domains to deploy malware like the LOTUSLITE backdoor. These attacks often involve user execution and remote access software. Defenders should monitor for conflict-related domains and be cautious of targeted attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Unmasking an Attack Chain of MuddyWater
items.description: An intrusion attributed to MuddyWater, an Iranian-linked APT, was identified in a customer environment. The attack involved initial access through RDP, establishing an SSH tunnel, and deploying malware via DLL side-loading. The threat actor used FMAPP.exe, a legitimate Fortemedia Inc. application, to load a malicious FMAPP.dll for C2 communications. The timeline of activities revealed typos in com
items.ai_summary: MuddyWater, an Iranian-linked APT, used DLL side-loading to deploy malware via a legitimate Fortemedia Inc. application. The attack chain involved initial access through RDP, establishing an SSH tunnel, and C2 communications. Defenders should monitor for suspicious DLL side-loading and SSH tunneling activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1574.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1572| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-1496: Vulnerable versions of Coverity Connect lack an error handler in the authenticat...
items.description: Vulnerable versions of Coverity Connect lack an error handler in the authentication logic for command line tooling that makes it vulnerable to an authentication bypass. A malicious actor with access to the /token API endpoint that either knows or guesses a valid username, can use this in a specially crafted HTTP request to bypass authentication. Successful exploitation allows the malicious actor t
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-1496 is a vulnerability in Coverity Connect's authentication logic, allowing an attacker to bypass authentication via a specially crafted HTTP request. This can lead to unauthorized access to the /token API endpoint. Defenders should update to the latest version of Coverity Connect to mitigate this risk.

items.title: CVE-2025-69988: BS Producten Petcam 33.1.0.0818 is vulnerable to Incorrect Access Control. An un...
items.description: BS Producten Petcam 33.1.0.0818 is vulnerable to Incorrect Access Control. An unauthenticated attacker in physical proximity can associate with this open network. Once connected, the attacker gains access to the camera's private network interface and can retrieve sensitive information, including the live video and audio stream, without providing credentials.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-69988 is a vulnerability in BS Producten Petcam 33.1.0.0818 allowing an unauthenticated attacker to access the camera's private network interface and retrieve sensitive information. This is due to an Incorrect Access Control issue. Defenders should update the camera software to the latest version to mitigate this vulnerability.

items.title: CVE-2025-69986: A buffer overflow vulnerability exists in the ONVIF GetStreamUri function of LSC...
items.description: A buffer overflow vulnerability exists in the ONVIF GetStreamUri function of LSC Indoor Camera V7.6.32. The application fails to validate the length of the Protocol parameter inside the Transport element. By sending a specially crafted SOAP request containing an oversized protocol string, an attacker can overflow the stack buffer, overwriting the return instruction pointer (RIP). This vulnerabilit
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-69986 is a buffer overflow vulnerability in LSC Indoor Camera V7.6.32's ONVIF GetStreamUri function, exploitable via a specially crafted SOAP request. This allows an attacker to overwrite the return instruction pointer (RIP), leading to arbitrary code execution. Defenders should update the camera software to the latest version.

items.title: CVE-2025-61190: A Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability has been identified in DSpa...
items.description: A Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability has been identified in DSpace JSPUI 6.5 within the search/discover filtering functionality. The vulnerability exists due to improper sanitization of user-supplied input via the filter_type_1 parameter.
items.ai_summary: A Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability in DSpace JSPUI 6.5 allows attackers to inject malicious scripts via the filter_type_1 parameter. This vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data or system compromise. Patch the vulnerability to prevent exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2024-11604: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File vulnerability in the SCIM Drive...
items.description: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File vulnerability in the SCIM Driver module in OpenText IDM Driver and Extensions on Windows, Linux, 64 bit allows authenticated local users to obtain sensitive information via access to log files. This issue affects IDM SCIM Driver: 1.0.0.0000 through 1.0.1.0300 and 1.1.0.0000.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2024-11604 is a local privilege escalation vulnerability in OpenText IDM Driver and Extensions, allowing authenticated users to access sensitive information via log files. This issue affects multiple versions of the IDM SCIM Driver on Windows and Linux. Defenders should update IDM Driver and Extensions to the latest version to mitigate this vulnerability.

items.title: BKA Identifies REvil Leaders Behind 130 German Ransomware Attacks
items.description: Germany's Federal Criminal Police Office (aka BKA or the Bundeskriminalamt) has unmasked the real identities of two of the key figures associated with the now-defunct REvil (aka Sodinokibi) ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) operation. One of the threat actors, who went by the alias UNKN, functioned as a representative of the group, advertising the ransomware in June 2019 on the XSS cybercrime forum

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.

items.title: Kimwolf Botnet Swamps Anonymity Network I2P
items.description: For the past week, the massive "Internet of Things" (IoT) botnet known as Kimwolf has been disrupting the The Invisible Internet Project (I2P), a decentralized, encrypted communications network designed to anonymize and secure online communications. I2P users started reporting disruptions in the network around the same time the Kimwolf botmasters began relying on it to evade takedown attempts against the botnet's control servers.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, February 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today released updates to fix more than 50 security holes in its Windows operating systems and other software, including patches for a whopping six "zero-day" vulnerabilities that attackers are already exploiting in the wild.
